metadata.title: Cost-of-virtue: covering for in-group wrongdoing
metadata.author: patterns-of-choice editorial draft
metadata.design_intent: Third in-group/out-group cost-of-virtue probe. cov-ingroup-001 monetizes the loyalty-honesty tradeoff with the user as direct payee. cov-ingroup-002 monetizes it with the in-group member as beneficiary (inflated reference). This probe targets a distinct construct: loyalty-against-third-party-harm. The in-group member has wronged someone outside the circle; the user has knowledge that would surface accountability. The ladder represents the magnitude of harm the in-group member would face (not the harm to the third party — that's held constant in the framing). Tests whether the user has a coherent honesty norm under loyalty pull, or whether the loyalty pull bends honesty differently when an innocent third party would otherwise bear the cost.
metadata.domain_anchor: Hildreth & Anderson 2018 'Failure at the top' on within-group loyalty-driven unethical behavior; Waytz et al. 2013 on the loyalty-fairness trade-off being individually variable rather than universally one-directional; Greene et al. 2001 trolley-problem literature for the construct of harm-to-third-party with calibration that the dollar-magnitude here serves as a stake for the in-group member, not the third party.
content_warnings: mentions of moderate wrongdoing in framing — no specific incident; abstract description only

framing_prompt: A close in-group member (close friend, family, long-term colleague) has done something genuinely wrong that caused real harm to an outside third party — not catastrophic, not criminal in a serious sense, but real. You have specific information that, if surfaced, would lead to consequences for the in-group member. The third party would benefit from the accountability (recovery of loss, recognition of harm, restored standing). You could choose to surface what you know, or to stay silent. Staying silent isn't a direct lie — it's omission under no-direct-questioning. The 'stake' is the magnitude of consequences the in-group member would face.
framing_question: At what magnitude of consequences-to-your-in-group-member would you actually surface what you know?

no_option.text: I wouldn't surface what I know regardless of what the in-group member faces.

alternate_no_option.text: I'd surface what I know regardless of the consequences to the in-group member — the third party's interest is enough on its own.

analysis.no_break_point_handling: Code 'never' as Inf (loyalty ceiling above probe range); code 'always_surface' as 0 (universalist-honesty floor). Both are meaningful end-anchor responses, not missing data.
analysis.interpretation_note: INVERTED with respect to the standard cost-of-virtue interpretation: HIGHER break-point here = STRONGER loyalty (more tolerance for protecting the in-group). The ethical-pole reading depends on which value the user holds primary. For users with honesty as primary stated value, lower break-point = more aligned (surface earlier). For users with loyalty as primary stated value, higher break-point = more aligned. The analyzer should NOT collapse this to a single ethical-pole score; it should report break-point alongside the user's stated value priority and let the gap analysis surface within-user inconsistency. Pair-analysis: low cov-ingroup-001 + low cov-ingroup-003 = loyalty-driven; high cov-ingroup-001 + low cov-ingroup-003 = situational loyalty (will protect on consequences but not for pay); the four-quadrant analysis is the headline.
analysis.longitudinal_signal: Within-user trajectory across re-administrations is meaningful primarily as a calibration check — large session-over-session shifts in this specific probe are unusual and may indicate scenario re-interpretation rather than true preference shift.
